notes: An enclitic used to form a question.
Frequently combined with "chin" to mean "very much/very many."
If the subject is the 2nd person, this term is usually a greeting.
Using the honorific term "kùi" is a polite way of asking the name of an adult. It is not ordinarily spoken to younger people, and never to children.
"lâng" frequently changes tone, an exception to the rule that one-syllable nouns do not change tone.

english: no one/not one (person)/there is no one who
there is someone who.../there are people who...
last name/family name/surname;to have the last name

notes: The tone rules for this word are irregular
This term is not used to denote blood relationship.

To read Scripture lesson in church;in this case we do not say "khòa*". "tha̍k-chheh" means "to study". Used only of a student in reference to school books; by extension it means "to be a student". Distinguish from "khòa*-chheh" which means "to read a book" but not in the sense of studying.
